speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two of the interview with Liz Manning, March 8, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was saying, I learned Hebrew, and I was speaking it pretty well by the time I was 12 years old. And when I came to Israel, in the way from-- in the way from the airport to the-- to the hotel, I was sitting in the taxi, and I could read all the street signs and all the things. But I couldn't read it freely because I had been disconnected from it. So I couldn't help myself, and I brought out my finger. And my finger was going like this in the taxi, and I became very embarrassed because I felt very stupid sitting there with that finger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- I sat on my hands. And I was sitting in the taxi, and I couldn't-- I wanted to read all the signs because it was interesting-- a butcher, a dress store, whatever, or with some, you know, some advertisements or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I couldn't-- I couldn't really read without my finger, and which had no connection, because-- and-- and I was forever jumping up and down on the taxi seat. So I was very unhappy because I-- when I was walked out in the street, I didn't understand what people were saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day we had-- we had-- my brother was at that time living in Israel. He had moved there from New York. And I was babysitting his two children. And I was in the house, and my aunt was coming, the-- my father's younger sister, Adele, had managed to get out of Vienna before we were all sent-- we were all deported in 1938.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she went on Israel on aliyah, and we don't know if they're illegals. And she-- she was there since 1938. So she was in an old age home at that time already in Israel. It was in 1970. And she was coming to visit my brother's house. It was before Shabbat. And we was putting chairs around the table to prepare for it. And one chair was a little chair. It was smaller than the other chairs, and I kept putting it in a place that was more convenient for me with the arrangement around the table. And she kept-- and the kids kept putting it back. And I said, this is--. And they said, no, no, it can't be there. And this is Tante-- Aunt-- Aunt Adele's chair. And I said-- and-- and they spoke Hebrew, and they didn't speak English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all of a sudden, I remembered the Ma Nishtana for Passover. What makes this night different from all the other night? Now, I didn't know. I knew a chair is a [HEBREW], right, because the nouns I was still pretty good with. So I said, [HEBREW]. So the children understood me, and they explained to me. They told with their hands, you know, Tante Adele is very little, and-- and this is her chair because we want this chair she can reach the floor and she doesn't have to sit up on top. So that was OK. So when my brother and his wife came home, I said, it's all right. It's all right. I can speak Hebrew. I just realized. I know all the prayers. I know what every word means, and I can translate it. I can speak Hebrew. And my sister-in-law starts laughing hysterically. And-- and I said, what's the matter?

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, just imagine yourself walking around New York and speaking Shakespearean English. That's what you will sound like if you speak this Hebrew what that you know. I said, I can't do that. So I came back to New York, and I enrolled in the [INAUDIBLE], and I learned how to speak it again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to celebrate Passover with your family before the war in Vienna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was-- it was a production. Everybody would come together. We would sit around. We-- we-- we were a very large family, and everybody-- third cousins, 15-- everybody was very, very close. But it was-- it was 99% my father's side. They were all very closely knit. And we would get together, and my father was the-- was the-- the head honcho. And my-- he-- he-- he was, you know, the old-fashioned thing. He had a kittel. He wore this linen garment and thing that you get when you get married, that you get buried in, and all that sort of thing. So he wore that with the pillows and the leather chair and then the whole production. And I would ask the questions. And then they would hide the afikomen. And I would make trades for what I got. And people were cooking for a week before. And everything was sanitized and-- and cleaned out and-- so that now no-- no bread crumbs were there. And I was fanatically going [? was ?] a little kid through all the corners. I was going to make sure our house was going to be the cleanest in the neighborhood, everything. We all sat down together. There would be sometimes-- well, I don't want to exaggerate. Maybe at certain weddings and-- and-- and-- and pigeon event, and we will have a larger thing, would be 90 people. But [INAUDIBLE] say there would be easily 60, 65 people getting together, people, wall-to-wall people, food, wall-to-wall food, and it was a fantastic thing. After the war, of the original 90, there were-- there were odd people outside, but in the country, there were six of us left-- my father, my brother, and I. Well, actually there was seven. But my-- my father's older sister, who was like a mother to me after my mother died, and her husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband's name-- she was-- she was Berta, which everybody called [? Tata, ?] and his name was Albert. And he was a very, very big man. And while we were in Theresienstadt, she was very concerned about his well-being. She was the-- she was-- I don't know, she was a fantastic woman. She took in her niece and made them-- was a mother for her. She was a mother to me. She-- she was a mother to all of us. She was a fantastic woman. And during-- during Theresienstadt, she was-- she was a little woman, and he was a very big, heavy man. And-- and she gave him her food, and she wound up with tuberculosis. She had a bad case of-- because she voluntarily starved herself so that he could eat. She was a saint. We don't have this in our religion, but she qualifies. Anyway, so that's what it was. And she was there and her husband and my father's youngest brother with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: His youngest brother's name was Samuel, and his wife was Liesl. And that was all that was left. And my father kept us all-- well, I'll tell you that later because my father kept us all alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was a very nice woman. She was a shy woman. She was not pretty. She was told she was nice-looking, nicely built, but she was not pretty. She was completely white because when she lost a child, her hair turned white overnight, which is really true. I only knew her with white hair. And she was a good cook, and she was a good mother, and that was her life. She didn't have any outside interests. Sometimes they went to-- went to a theater, or they went out to dinner, but she-- she-- she lived with her family. Every Sunday she was there, and during the week sometimes she'd meet a girlfriend in the afternoon. But she went to the market every morning with the market basket and bought fresh food. And then she spent three hours cooking dinner. The big meal was eaten between 1:00 and-- and-- and 3:00. You know, we ate at, like, 1:30. My father came home from work. And then we went-- we-- when-- when we were older, after 10, you go to school from 1:00 to 3:00 also. But before that, you only went until 1:00, and there was no school in the afternoon until 10. And my father went back to work, and we went back to school. And she sometimes would see a girlfriend. But she had-- her life was in her family. That was it. She was-- I guess she was an unhappy woman, because her-- she was very shy. I don't know whether she was-- she was not unhappy. She was sort of-- had sort of a-- sort-- sort of a sad edge to her because her mother had cheated her husband out of something she had promised, and she felt that--. Well, I-- I would have felt it if this happened to me, that-- that I'm here under false pretenses. And-- and-- and-- and they were all such a tightly knit clan, you know. Nobody ever did anything. But she wasn't one of them because she wasn't as outgoing as they were, you know, and she was-- she was very nice. I just-- I just started to establish a relationship with her. I would tell her that-- you know, I was like 14 years old. I would tell her she should use a little lipstick, and when-- when-- when she was taken from us, in a very horrible way-- we'll get to that later. [INAUDIBLE] I want to start. I remember-- I can tell you something about--. So we lived our life, and it was OK. And one day, you know, the-- the-- the-- in 1938 everything-- they-- they-- they-- they started to-- you know, Hitler with his business. In 1935 the Nuremberg laws was passed. But I had two uncles that lived in Germany, in eastern Germany in Leipzig, two brothers of my father. But since they had Austrian passports, they were not affected by the Jewish laws, by the Nuremberg laws, because they were foreigners. They had foreign passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when in 1938, when finally the-- this had been going on. Somebody was-- they brought in a government that Hitler approved of. And the Austrian government was thinking, in the couple of years before, they had murdered the Austrian chancellor, the-- the Nazis. And-- and things went on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, in-- in March the 12th, I think it was, the 12th or the 13th of March, 1938-- it was Friday the 12th, I think, that they-- they started marching. And the Austrian-- you know, to take over Austria. And the Austrian chancellor went on the radio, and he said, I-- I-- I-- I cede to the superior power. That's what he said. I'm translating because he, of course, said it not in English. And-- and I-- at that time, my-- my father was in the habit of having a glass of fresh draft beer with his evening meal. And I was just-- had gone next door to get him one, a-- a-- a glass of beer. And they had the radio on. It was Friday night. We didn't have a radio on, so we didn't hear it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they had the radio on in the store, and I-- and I heard him say this. And I came back in, and I told him, Papa, I just heard Chancellor Schuschnigg, who was the Austrian chancellor, told that speech, and he said he's giving up for the superior power. And my father said, that's the end of us all. That's what he said. Had you been hearing about what was going on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, of course. I had two uncles that lived there. But I was 12 years old. This was not something that was in the forefront of my awareness because I was a child. I was going to school. I was busy. I was learning English. I was learning Spanish. I was taking-- I-- I was-- I was beginning to take Latin because I wanted to study medicine. I was learning Hebrew. I was going to-- to to-- to Beth Yakov. I was busy with my girlfriends, curling my hair, which was as straight as an arrow, and-- and-- and-- and doing things. I-- I wasn't involved. I heard things talking. People were talking, but nobody discussed it-- nobody discussed politics with a 12-year-old child. There was no television. Radio was used for entertainment. There were no political commentators. I read the newspapers from the time I was five years old. But I read the cartoons, and I read the human-interest stories. But I didn't read politics. I didn't know what they were talking about.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the Jewish heritage something that you were proud of?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish heritage was part of my life. I was very proud of, number one, of where I was coming from. I was very proud that-- I could see around me that in school, the Jewish children got better marks than the other kids. I was very proud of the fact that we were-- that we were good people, that we were nice people, that this was the religion that the whole world was living by, even though they-- they-- they turned it around a little bit. They-- they put the whipped cream on it, and they called it something else. But it was the same thing. It had no-- there was no difference. And I knew all of these things. They were explained to me, and I understood them. I had read books about them, and I was very into it. And I was very religious. I-- I mean, I was very religious. I did what I-- I went to the youth services every Sunday. As a matter of fact, the-- the rabbi of the temple where I was attending services, which was about a block and a half from the apartment where I lived-- where we lived until I was nine years old. And then it was, like, three blocks from where we moved to in 1935. His name was Murmelstein. And he wound up-- I wound up coming across him later in life. He was the head of the Judenrat in Theresienstadt when I was there in concentration camp. And so-- so I was involved with my life. I didn't have-- I was very-- I could sing all the songs and say all the prayers. And I prayed every morning and I prayed every night before I went to bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the only thing I didn't do was when I saw the movies. They were all kneeling down. And I said to my mother, why don't we kneel down? [? I ?] says, because we are Jewish. We don't do that. I said, OK. So what was it like at first to hear the Nazi doctrine about Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] What was it like? It was a nightmare because, number one, on Friday night, the street that I lived in a side street of was one of the main entry points leading to the inner city in Vienna. where-- where they were coming in. And people were standing 10 deep on the street, yelling, Heil Hitler. The population of Vienna was out in force, and they were all welcoming him. It was an enthusiastic reception. They were all very, very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Austria is the hot-bed of anti-Semitism. I don't know if you're aware of it. About 85% of the concentration camp personnel, the-- the-- the movers and the shakers-- maybe not all of them-- but even the-- this lesser personnel, they were-- it was much easier for them to-- to-- to be mean to the Jews and to-- to-- to-- to-- I don't know, kill Jews? Even in the beginning, to-- to-- to be-- and they were-- they were dyed-in-the-wool anti-Semites.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is the-- that was the-- a Jew-- even before, from after the Social Democrats went out in 1932, we had a sort of a little revolution. The Christian Socialist Party, a Jew could not have a state position. A Jew could not be a policeman or a teacher or anything like that. So there-- there was already prejudice for-- against Jews in Austria before that. All the schools were Catholic. All the children said 'Our Father' when they started to. Every classroom had a crucifix in it. That was life. So that was nothing special. But then, when they came, they-- they were very-- it was-- it was horrible because they-- they had painted slogans with the takeover. They had painted slogans in paint, in yellow paint, all over the streets. And they made Jewish people, especially somebody that looked like they were Hassidim, with beards or-- or traditional Jewish garb, they made them-- and young girls too. A neighbor I had, she just told me two-- two weeks ago that-- that she was scrubbing streets. But they made them clean the streets with toothbrushes. Take the paint off. It was-- and they-- they-- tore him around [INAUDIBLE] It was-- Hieronymous Bosch. It was a terrible, terrible nightmare that was going on all us. And I remember in-- in-- in-- my father was a temperamental man. He had a very bad temper. He would, woo, woo, woo, woo-- scream. And my mother would say, yell a little louder. They can't hear you three streets away. And then he would say, I don't want to. And he would start laughing and realize how ridiculous he was, and then there was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the 10th of November was and this-- but they're burning all the books and everything. I remember I had a girlfriend. Do you want her name? Her name was Gerte Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she was-- she had blue eyes and two long blonde braids. So when my father-- they were going around, and they were taking over all the Jewish store. Whatever they hadn't been taken out-- over yet, they were taking out. My mother was-- My father said, nobody's going to come after me. And he took one of the-- what you call it, a chisel. And he took a chisel, and he sharpened it [INAUDIBLE] like a razor. It had a very sharp edge, and he put that in his pocket. And he says, when one of them comes after me, nobody's going to get me. I'm going to take a few of them with me. And he walked out with this. And when my mother saw what the things that were going on in the street, she wanted to be sure that my father didn't get himself killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she sent me-- we were 12 years old-- and my girlfriend Gerte to-- to-- to go to my father's workplace, to his studio, to his work studio, and to-- to make sure that--. You know, she said, if he sees you there, he's not going to kill anybody, so they won't kill him. He'll be quiet. So we went there. And we-- we were-- we-- it was-- it was horrible. We saw books burning and people being hit and beaten, and-- and-- but I was told to go, and I did what I was told. I mean, there was no playing around. There was no games. It needed to be done.

speaker: interviewee
text: I still do it. If I need something needs to be done, even if I can't do it, I make myself do it because it needs to be done. And I did it then too. And we went there, and-- and-- and there was nobody there. My father-- it was already-- it was closed. There was nobody there. So we-- we-- we-- it took us four hours to get home because we were afraid to go on the car, because sometimes they saw somebody that was Jewish, and then they would rocking the trolley cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- so we walked. Anyway, it turned out somebody had walked into my father's store, and somebody had-- whom he had known all his life. And he was very surprised that-- that my father's name was on the list.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, look, he says, I didn't know you were a Jew. He says, I don't have anything. I have orders to do that. Do me a favor. Don't make-- don't make me treat you like I don't want to. And my father, he said, you know, he wasn't going to kill somebody that he had known all his life. So he let him-- he gave him the keys, and he walked out of his business. So shortly after we came home, my father came home, and he told us that story. That was that the-- the-- the 10th of November, 1938, Crystal Night.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the changes that occurred after the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were all thrown out of school. In 1939, we were told-- my father-- in 1938, my father lost his business. In 1939, we were that we had to give up our apartment. Somebody gave us the equivalent of 300 marks, which is for the-- for the whole furniture because she wanted to have a-- a piece of paper that she had bought it. So to give a whole home full of furniture, which was all custom-made-- my father had made it himself. I had a-- a doll furniture which was an exact replica of my parents' bedroom-- the bed, the closet. They didn't have built-in closets. You had, like, chifferobes, you know, out the-- and-- and-- the-- the-- the nightstand, it was an exact mahogany with-- with-- with the blonde wood moldings. It was a beautiful, beautiful set. I had that. Everything was left, everything. We had to-- we could to take our clothes and some bedding and things. Not even furniture, not-- not-- bedding, linens, and blankets, but not mattresses and not beds. We had to just leave everything behind. And so we-- we got another apartment. Well, it wasn't really an apartment. It was a building next to the big temple in Vienna where they had sort of an office building there, where they had schools of Talmud, Torah, and other things. So they used some of the offices, and they made homes out of them. There were a couple of big apartments in that building, you know, like eight-, nine-room apartments like they used to have at that time. And they-- they-- they gave everybody a bedroom. Each family had a bedroom. And there were all the families that were in this apartment would use one kitchen. And that's how we lived from 1939 until-- we had two rooms, one living room-size sort of room and one little room, like a one-window room. And in one-- in the big room, there was in one corner, there was a-- a a cook-- a wooden cook stove, and that was our kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How soon after the Nazi occupation were you kicked out of school? Was it immediate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pretty much. Well, actually, it was-- I don't know because I didn't finish the year because I caught scarlet fever, and I was in the hospital. So I don't know whether they were in school until June or not. I know in April I stopped going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was very, very soon after that. I remember the-- the teacher saying something about the Jews. You know, even at 12. That was horrible. I remember saying to her, we want to leave, but you're not letting us go. If you don't like us, why don't you let us go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, you see, children, this is what Jews are. You cannot talk to them. They always have a fresh answer. I remember her name. It was von [? Krasnik ?], I hope she died in pain soon after. Anyway--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to hear these kinds of things out of authority figures?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very, very-- I didn't realize it at that time. But when I got older and I understood, I realized that we were all terribly damaged because we had all been taught. It was insidious. It was-- we had all been taught that authority you listen to, you respect. They know better than you. When you're a grown-up, you will know as well as they do. You'll become an equal, and then you can tell other people how to behave too. You'll tell your own children. But right now, you have to follow the rules because that's how people live in a civilized society. God wants you to get along with everybody, and that's how people live. And all of a sudden, everybody tells you that you come of an inferior stock, that you're like vermin, and that you-- I knew it wasn't true. I understood it fully, consciously. But it must have worked on my mind. I-- it stands to reason that there is no way that this doesn't work on you and this doesn't influence you. I actually, personally-- I wouldn't admit it, even to myself, but I was ashamed to be Jewish, until the Six-Day War, when all of a sudden, they were not lambs that went to the slaughter and allowed themselves to be killed. They were not submissive. They didn't say, yes, sir. They didn't kowtow. They didn't say, don't make waves. If somebody hits me over the head, I'm going to hit him back, and I'm not going to worry if he's going to hit me tomorrow or not. I can't stand the idea that he's not entitled. I don't think another human being is entitled to decide whether or not I'm entitled to live. It's not his-- so I understood that then, but--. I'm sorry. I'm getting angry. I'm getting too involved. No, seriously. I-- it was very difficult because it was working on me. I was told-- I was ashamed. I wouldn't wear anything. I remember another terrible thing. It just came-- popped into my mind. I'm going to do it out of context. When I came back from Theresienstadt after the war, we didn't have anything, and I came back in a-- in a-- in a cattle car. That was the only type of transportation available. And we had a typhoid epidemic, so I didn't really come back until July. Even though the war was over on May 8, we didn't come back until July to Vienna. They-- they got to [? gather ?] [INAUDIBLE]. But we had mattresses in there, and there were only about 12 people instead of 70. And we stopped every two hours so that we could go to toilet.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was a different thing. But it was still a cattle car. I was still lying on a mattress on the floor. That's how I came back. To me this was princely. I was sleeping on a mattress, because I had slept on straw for two years-- I didn't have in Theresienstadt, but on the third floor with bed bugs. But on the third level, I meant. But-- I'm sorry, what was I telling you? [INAUDIBLE] You were talking about coming back to Vienna from-- from Theresienstadt on the-- on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, I know. All right. So one day in July, in the end of July, there was a-- a movie. It was called [GERMAN ], where they showed pictures, movies, of concentration camp liberations and what they found. And I went with a couple of friends. And when we came out, I remember to this day. I said to my friends, I've just been there. I said, nobody's going to believe it. I've just been there, and I don't believe it. Nobody's going to believe this is true. It took me 50 years, but now nobody is telling you that this is true.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this-- this unconscious sense of shame was something that began with the Nazi occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: It began with the Nazi-- it was insidious. It worked on me. I didn't really-- I was belligerently Jewish, but I never displayed any sign of Jewishness. There's something else that I did which was very stupid. I'll get to it in a minute. I-- it has to do with-- with wearing the Jewish star. But that was stupidity and belligerence, but I did it anyway. But I didn't display-- I-- I had the mezuzah that I was wearing. And I never-- I always wore it under my clothing because I didn't want to advertise it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I know that everybody would hate me as soon as they would see it because it was true. They did, even when the government didn't tell them to. Jews are looked down upon in Vienna to this very day. They-- they found Waldheim and they-- they put him in there. They don't like us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have non-Jewish friends before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No-- yes. I had non-Jewish acquaintances. Sometimes I went to somebody's house or something. But their parents weren't too keen on letting their children play with us. And my parents weren't too keen. And most of the people I-- most of the girlfriends I had were Jewish. There was one that was not. She was sort of in the group. But her mother didn't have a husband, so she wasn't really--

speaker: interviewer
text: We We have to stop the tape now and change it. Excuse me.
